title: Bammy
subtitle: Jamaican cassava flatbread
excerpt: Grated cassava pressed, salted, and cooked into thick rounds that are soaked in coconut milk and then pan-fried until lightly golden. The result is a soft, slightly dense bread with a mild coconut flavour that pairs with fried fish and escovitch. Bammy is one of the oldest foods in Jamaica, predating European contact, made by the indigenous Taíno people from cassava.
sourceNotes: Bammy is of Taíno indigenous origin, made from bitter cassava that was grated and pressed to remove the prussic acid before cooking. The technique of pressing and heating cassava flatbread is documented from pre-Columbian Jamaica. Modern bammy uses sweet cassava (lower cyanogen content) and is sold commercially in Jamaica, though home production from grated fresh cassava is the traditional method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pressed and dried cassava rounds can be made ahead. Soak and fry just before serving.

content.text: Use sweet cassava (yuca), not bitter cassava. Modern sweet cassava from Caribbean food shops has a much lower cyanogen content than traditional bitter cassava and does not require the traditional detoxification process. Peel the cassava completely, removing both the rough outer skin and the thin pink layer beneath it.

The cassava must be pressed to remove as much liquid as possible before cooking. Squeeze the grated cassava in a clean cloth very firmly — the starch that remains in the cloth is what holds the bammy together. The wetter the grated cassava, the more the bammy falls apart in the pan.

Grate the peeled {{cassava}} coarsely. Place in a clean cloth and squeeze as hard as possible to remove the liquid. Tip the pressed cassava into a bowl, add the {{sea-salt-fine}}, and mix well. Divide into 4 portions and press each into a flat round about 10 cm across and 1.5 cm thick, compressing firmly so it holds together. Heat a dry frying pan (no oil) over medium heat. Cook each round for 4 to 5 minutes per side until a light brown crust forms and the cassava has set. Remove and cool slightly.

Dip each cooked round briefly in the {{coconut-milk}}, letting it soak for about 30 seconds per side. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in the frying pan over medium heat. Fry the soaked bammies for 3 to 4 minutes per side until golden and lightly crisped on the outside. Serve immediately alongside fried fish or escovitch.

items.symptom: Bammy falls apart during first cook
items.fix: Squeeze the cassava in the cloth a second time. The pressed cassava should feel almost dry to the touch
Press to no more than 1.5 cm. Thicker bammies cook through unevenly

items.cause: Cassava has a very mild flavour — the coconut soak is the seasoning
items.fix: Use full-fat coconut milk for the soak. Light coconut milk does not add enough flavour

content.text: Bammy is one of the few foods in Jamaica with a direct line to the pre-colonial indigenous population. The Taíno people made cassava flatbread across the Caribbean and Central America — a technology that survived European colonisation and the near-destruction of indigenous culture in Jamaica. Modern bammies are commercially produced and widely sold in Jamaica, soaked in coconut milk rather than the original water-soaking method. They are served primarily with fried fish, a pairing as fixed as chips and fish in Britain.
